coredata.dc:title: Tunable photoluminescence emission from surface-state to carbon core-state of PAMAM carbonized polymer dots and its high-sensitive detection of copper(II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xiaoyi
Zhang, Shangshang
Shi, Weiguang
Cheng, Dan
Li, Hong-Wei
Xu, Shihan
Dong, Biao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are important zero-dimensional materials with excellent luminescent properties. However, the mechanism of CDs photoluminescence is still an open debate, which significantly hinders the development of CDs with desired optical properties. In this work, bright blue fluorescent carbonized 1.0 G polyamine-amine polymer dots (NCPDs) has been prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, with a quantum yield of 57 %. The dominant luminescence of the NCPDs is attributed to the surface states based on synergistic hybridization between the carbon backbones and connected functional groups at below 120 °C. Photoluminescence transforms from the surface states to the surface - carbon core states, and then to carbon core states from 120° to 150 °C. Meanwhile, the degree of carbonization increases along with the size of NCPDs growing from 1.36 to 3.20 nm, and the NCPDs present excitation-dependent emission properties. Due to the remarkable fluorescence property of NCPDs, Cu2+ determination has been carried out in the aqueous phase, and the detection limit is 9 nM, which is much lower than the international minimum standard (20 μM). NCPDs present high selectivity and sensitivity toward detecting Cu2+, and provide potential applications in detecting metal pollution in the environmental water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
PAMAM
Surface state fluorescence
Carbon core state fluorescence
Copper ionic detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The composition and the possible fluorescence mechanism of NCPDs
The influencing factors on the fluorescence properties of NCPDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82073475 ), Major Basic Research Projects of Shandong Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2020ZD36 ) and the Graduate Student Innovation Research Project of Northeast Petroleum University ( JYCX_CX03_2018 ). We are grateful to the research group of Xiaoyang Liu (Jilin University), microscopic test center of the Northeast Petroleum University for characterization, and the Daqing Ecological Environment Monitoring Center for environmental assessment.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82073475), Major Basic Research Projects of Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2020ZD36) and the Graduate Student Innovation Research Project of Northeast Petroleum University (JYCX_CX03_2018). We are grateful to the research group of Xiaoyang Liu (Jilin University), microscopic test center of the Northeast Petroleum University for characterization, and the Daqing Ecological Environment Monitoring Center for environmental assessment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence emission spectra of the blank control groups and NCPDs. On the left is the schematic of the reaction, and the inset shows the photographs of them under 365 nm UV light.

The effect of synthesis temperature on the average particle size and emission spectra of NCPDs. (a), (b) and (c) are the structural schematics and TEM images of the products synthesized at 100 ℃, 120 ℃, and 150 ℃, respectively. (d) and (e) are the emission spectra of the products synthesized at 120 ℃ (λEX = 380 nm) and 150 ℃ (λEX = 450 nm).

The fluorescence excitation spectra of NCPDs synthesized at (a) different reaction temperatures, (b) different time, (c) the excitation peak fitting at different temperatures, and at the condition of T = 120 ℃, nPAMAM: nNa3Cit = 1:4, pH= 5, t = 120 min, (d) excitation-emission mapping, (e) fluorescence emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths, and (f) fluorescence lifetime.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of NCPDs, pure 1.0 G PAMAM and Na3Cit, high-resolution C1s (b) and N1s (c) spectra of NCPDs.

The concentration quenching (a) and the ions induced fluorescence quenching (b, the concentration of NCPDs was 3 mM) of NCPDs. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions, and the inset shows its linear regression (the concentration of NCPDs was 2 mM).
(a) Zeta potential, (b) particle size, and (c) fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs before and after Cu2+ quenching, (d) schematic mechanism of Cu2+ quenching.

head.ce:title: Tunable photoluminescence emission from surface-state to carbon core-state of PAMAM carbonized polymer dots and its high-sensitive detection of copper(II)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are important zero-dimensional materials with excellent luminescent properties. However, the mechanism of CDs photoluminescence is still an open debate, which significantly hinders the development of CDs with desired optical properties. In this work, bright blue fluorescent carbonized 1.0 G polyamine-amine polymer dots (NCPDs) has been prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, with a quantum yield of 57 %. The dominant luminescence of the NCPDs is attributed to the surface states based on synergistic hybridization between the carbon backbones and connected functional groups at below 120 °C. Photoluminescence transforms from the surface states to the surface - carbon core states, and then to carbon core states from 120° to 150 °C. Meanwhile, the degree of carbonization increases along with the size of NCPDs growing from 1.36 to 3.20 nm, and the NCPDs present excitation-dependent emission properties. Due to the remarkable fluorescence property of NCPDs, Cu2+ determination has been carried out in the aqueous phase, and the detection limit is 9 nM, which is much lower than the international minimum standard (20 μM). NCPDs present high selectivity and sensitivity toward detecting Cu2+, and provide potential applications in detecting metal pollution in the environmental water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more interests due to their environmentally friendly feature [1,2], low toxicity [3,4], and high fluorescence quantum yields [5]. Compared to traditional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have broad and continuous excitation, while narrow and symmetrical emission bands. The fluorescence diversity of CDs is caused by their complicated structures and components, including different molecules, oligomers, polymer chains, polymer clusters, and incompletely carbonized carbon cores. Three kinds of photoluminescence (PL) origins of CDs have been proposed, which are carbon core states, surface states, and molecular fluorescence, respectively. Among them, the surface states-derived PL emission correlates with the synergistic effect of the carbon backbones and the connected functional groups have been considered as the main luminescence origins of CDs. When the reaction temperature is over 150 °C, fluorophores begin to consume and further form the carbon core, and the PL occurs because of the synergistic effect of the carbogenic core and the molecule state[6–8]. Although great achievements have been made in CDs preparations and applications, the actual mechanism of their PL remains a matter under debate, which heavily hinders the controllable preparation with desired optical properties and clear structures [9]. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to establish the relationship between their structure and property in CDs.

Based on carbon core structures and surface groups, CDs can be classified into three different categories: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [10,11]. Among them, the CPDs contain a carbon core with aggregated or cross-linked functional groups/polymer chains attached to the surface [12]. Therefore, their optical properties are dominated by the surface state, the molecular state, the core structure, and the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect, respectively [13].

Polyamide-amine (PAMAM), a symmetrical macromolecule with internal cavities, exhibits good remarkable compatibility and easy modification properties [14,15]. Recent studies indicated that excellent fluorescence could be obtained from PAMAM-based CPDs by modifying the surface with the designed terminal groups, including -NH2 or -OH from internal reactions, such as the oxidation of secondary or tertiary amine groups, crosslinking, and the aggregation of internal carbonyl groups [16,17]. Thus, PAMAM-based CPDs are suitable candidates to investigate unclear fluorescent mechanisms on core-shell structural CDs and their fluorescent properties. Furthermore, the multi-terminal structure has gained interests in the field of ion detection.

During the past decades, the rapid development of industrialization has led to a large increase of harmful heavy metal ions, including Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, etc., causing serious environmental problems and posing a threat to humans [18,19]. Among them, Cu2+ is considered a carcinogen with the ability to damage DNA, which is highly toxic to all living organisms and induces neurological disorders, diabetes, hemorrhaging, and kidney disorders [20]. The current detection techniques for copper ions have common disadvantages, including high cost of equipment, time-consuming detection, and low sensitivity, limiting the application in monitoring environments. Based on the good fluorescence and high selectivity, PAMAM-based CPDs can be applied in Cu2+ detection in the field of environmental and life sciences with their good compatibility, nontoxicity, and easy surface modification. Moreover, compared to the current popular fluorescent probes based on organic molecules, PAMAM-based CPDs could be an excellent candidate as a fluorescent sensor with improved detection performance for metal ions because CPDs are hydrophilic, non-toxic, and rich nitrogen-containing functional groups, resulting in high detection sensitivity and without environmental pollution [21–23].

In this work, PAMAM-based CPDs with a bright blue fluorescence was synthesized by a facile and eco-friendly hydrothermal method, where employing sodium citrate (Na3Cit) and 1.0-generation poly (amidoamine) (1.0 G PAMAM-NH2) as precursors, named NCPDs. Compared with a single carbon source, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs was increased significantly, and the quantum yield (QY) was about 57 % (selecting quinine sulfate as the standard). When the prepared temperature of NCPDs is below 120 ℃, excitation-independent emission can be observed, suggesting that the PL emission of NCPDs is a typical origin from the surface state and the energy bands are dependent on the surface groups and structures but not on the particle size. With increasing the reaction temperature, a high degree of carbonization NCPDs is obtained with excitation-dependent emission property. The results indicate that the transformation process of their PL originated from the surface states to the surface - carbon core states, and then to carbon core states, which is related to the reaction temperature and time, and the degree of carbonization. In particularly, the carbon cores with a high degree of carbonization are related to the excitation-dependent emission. NCPDs have many potential applications due to their high stability and quantum yield. This new type of NCPDs with surface and carbon core states is an important model for studying luminescence mechanism, as an important copper ion probe with high selectivity and sensitivity for environmental monitoring, and also a remarkable candidate of high temperature sensing materials.

Ethylene diamine (99 %), methyl methacrylate (98 %), methanol (99.9 %), sodium citrate (98 %), quinine sulfate (98 %), hydrochloric acid (35 wt%), sodium hydroxide (98 %), H3BO3 (AR), H3PO4 (AR), KCl (AR), NaCl (AR), CaCl2 (AR), CdCl3 (AR), CuCl2 (AR), CoCl2 (AR), NiCl2 (AR) and HgCl2 (AR) were purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The buffer solutions of different pH values were prepared with 0.04 M H3PO4-H3BO3 mixed acid solution and a sodium hydroxide solution (0.2 M). 1.0 G PAMAM-NH2 was synthesized according to the method described in our previous literature [23]. 1.0 G PAMAM-NH2 was configured with a solution of 200 M. The sodium citrate was dissolved in water to form solutions of 80 M and 1000 M. All organic reagents were re-purified before use.

The NCPDs were synthesized by heating and stirring a certain concentration of 1.0 G PAMAM-NH2 and sodium citrate at a certain pH. Reaction time, temperature, pH, and reactant ratio were adjusted individually to obtain the most suitable condition. The concentration of 1.0 G PAMAM-NH2 in the reaction system was kept at 4 mM all the time. The pH values of the reaction system were adjusted with the amount of HCl (aq) or NaOH (aq), and the errors were controlled within ± 0.05.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained by using a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, MA). The slits of excitation and emission were 10.0 nm and 2.5 nm respectively, with a scanning rate of 200 nm·min−1. The morphology and the structure of the sample were analyzed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin, FEI, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV spectrophotometer (UV–vis) (UV-1700 PharmaSpec, China). The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra analysis was carried out by a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Tensor27, BRUKER, Germany) in the 400–4,000 cm−1 region. For the preparation of FT-IR samples, the as-prepared PDs were extracted from the aqueous phase by the incompatibility with acetone. The extractive was collected by centrifugation and then dried in a vacuum drying oven at 35 °C. The samples were coated in pelleted KBr sheets during the infrared test. The nuclear magnetic resonance (1 H NMR) spectra were collected on a Bruker AV400 (400 MHz) (Bryker, Germany) with dried samples as shown in above which were dissolved in D2O. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with the ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer using 300 W AlKα radiation (VG Scientific, UK). The base pressure was about 3 × 10−9 mbar. The binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.8 eV from adventitious carbon. The size of the sample were measured with atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8, American) in tapping-mode using a Brooker scanning probe microscope and Nano IVa controller. The zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter were estimated at 25 °C (Malvern Nano ZS, UK). Fluorescence lifetime was measured on transient and steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, QM8000, Canada). The pH values of the reaction system were monitored with a pH meter (PHS-3E, Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd, China).

The QY of NCPDs (in water) was calculated by a reference point method, in which quinine sulfate were chosen as standards [24]. In this experiment, quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY=54 %) were chosen as standards, and the QY of NCPDs (in water) was calculated by reference point method:
ϕ
x
= (
A
n
/
A
x
) ( ∫
F
x
/
F
n
)
ϕ
n
Where ϕ is the QY, A is the absorbance (0.01 <A<0.05, in order to minimize reabsorption effects), and ∫F is the integrated area of the emission spectrum. The subscript “n” and “x” refer to standard with known QY and the NCPDs sample, respectively.

In order to explore the selectivity of the fluorescent NCPDs on sensing metal ions, some common alkali metals cations and some low-valence transition metal ions were tested. 20 mM various metal ions were prepared by adding NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, CaCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, HgCl2 and NiCl2, separately. The probe was 3 mM solution of NCPDs, calculated by the concentration of PAMAM in the solution. Ignoring the change in the volume of the solution, the fluorescent spectra were recorded by adding a series of metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+) or deionized water with the same volume (0.2 mL) as a control. The 2 mM NCPDs was selected for establishing the standard curve. A series of Cu2+ solutions were prepared as precursor at the concentrations of 0.125 mM, 0.25 mM, 0.5 mM, 0.75 mM, 1 mM, 1.25 mM, 1.5 mM, 1.75 mM, and 2 mM, respectively. Ignoring the change in the volume of the solution, the fluorescent spectra were recorded by adding 0.2 mL the above-mentioned precursor.

The NCPDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method at different conditions. Compared with a single carbon sources, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs was increased significantly with a brand excitation from 280 to 410 nm and a fluorescence emission of 441 nm ( Fig. 1), and the quantum yield was determined to be around 57 %. As shown in Fig. S1, only amorphous particles with a maximum size of 1.9 nm and an average size of 1.0 nm can be observed in the AFM and the TEM image, respectively. Also, the fluorescence emission was very weak at 90 ℃. Upon the temperature increased, the particle size regularly increased (1.40 nm, 1.60 nm, 1.36 nm, 2.05 nm, 3.00 nm, and 3.20 nm at 100 ℃, 110 ℃, 120 ℃, 130 ℃, 140 ℃, and 150 ℃, respectively.) ( Fig. 2), and well-resolved lattice fringes could be observed. When the temperature reached 120 ℃, the average particle size suddenly decreased to 1.36 nm, and well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.217 nm were observed (Fig. 2b), which are close to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite carbon. The NCPDs showed excitation-independent emission at 441 nm by varying the excitation wavelength from 280 to 410 nm (Fig. 2d). When the temperature rised, there was a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation, and the average particle size increased gradually. With the further carbonization, the PL emission of NCPDs was red-shifted with excitation-dependent emission, and the average particle size was increased to be 3.20 nm, together with a maximum size of 6.10 nm (Fig. 2c, S1f and S1h). By varying the excitation wavelength from 420 to 520 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission of the carbogenic NCPDs was red-shifted from 500 to 540 nm (Fig. 2e). These results showed that the carbon core affected the fluorescence properties directly. The appearance of carbon cores provided a fixed position for potential fluorescence functional groups, which induced some rigidity in the surface structure, and significantly improved the fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the increasing particle sizes led to different chemical properties of carbogenic NCPDs.

In order to improve the fluorescence properties of NCPDs, optimization experiments were performed by adjusting the following factors: reaction temperature, material ratios, initial pH value and reaction time (Table S1). As described in Fig. S2, each reaction condition can affect the reaction progress enormously, and the fluorescent properties of the products have dependence on time and temperature. Interestingly, the fluorescence excitation spectra showed that the non-gauss symmetric emission peaks were located between 300 and 400 nm at lower reaction temperatures (<120 ℃). When the temperature increased continually, two different excitation peaks could be observed ( Fig. 3a). Besides, with the extension of the response time, the degree of carbonization and the size of carbon cores gradually increased (Fig. 3b), and NCPDs transformed into carbogenic CDs. The pH also affected the activity of the reactants when adjusting the concentration of protons in the reaction system (Fig. S2b). As the concentration of Na3Cit in the reaction system continued to rise, the reaction became more complete (higher degree of carbonization, Fig. S2c). In addition, the interaction between molecules also affected the symmetry of the emission peak. Therefore, the transition point of the process had been selected as the optimal synthesis condition to prepare NCPDs (T = 120 ℃, nPAMAM: nNa3Cit = 1:4, pH=5, t = 120 min). Besides, there was an obvious change in the UV-Vis absorption spectra of NCPDs with different reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, with deepening the reaction degree, the peak at 236 nm was gradually generated, and the peak at 350 nm gradually increased.

For exploring the effect of reaction conditions on the position of the excitation peaks, the excitation spectra of the products were fitted with two peaks by the software Peakfit (R2 >0.98), which were labeled as a and b (Fig. 3c). With the temperature increase, peak a gradually blue-shifted, while peak b gradually red-shifted, and their intensities increased first and then decreased. The two main excitation peaks suggested that there were two main fluorescence excitation sites in NCPDs. The one with high excitation energy corresponds to peak a, and the other with low excitation energy corresponds to peak b. In general, the PL emissions of NCPDs are originated from surface states and/or carbon core states. Due to its size, chemical structure (containing π-π conjugated system), or lattice defects, the fluorescence emission of carbon cores requires higher excitation energy. The surface states contain a large number of surface defects formed by sub-fluorescent groups, which are mainly n-π conjugation and the electron transition requires lower energy. At the condition of T = 120 ℃, nPAMAM: nNa3Cit = 1:4, pH= 5, t = 120 min, NCPDs emitted a blue luminescence that excited in a wide range of 260–410 nm. Two different intensity ranges of 340–390 nm and below 320 nm were recorded in the excitation-emission mapping (Fig. 3d and e). These observations suggested that two fluorescence excitation sites represented the fluorescence emission of the carbon cores and the carbon cores, associating with the excitation-independent emission property. Furthermore, UV-Vis absorption spectra showed that a strong absorption peak (K-band) at 236 nm, which belonged to the π→π * transition of sp2-hybrid carbon, suggesting the existence of pyrrole structure in NCPDs (Fig. S4). The absorption peak (R-band) at around 350 nm was attributed to the transition from the unbonded lone pair electrons to π antibonding orbital of in the heteroatom (N or O) of amide or carboxylic acid (n→π * transition). Also, the fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs were 7.70 ns and 7.58 ns with λEX = 380 nm and λEX = 320 nm, respectively. Almost equivalent fluorescence lifetime illustrate that the PL emissions of NCPDs originated from a uniform sturcture of surface states and carbon core states (Fig. 3f). Therefore, peak a belonged to the well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing carbon core states with higher excitation energy, while peak b could be assigned to the n-π conjugation of PAMAM polymer surface states with lower energy [25–28]. Interestingly, two fluorescence excitation sites NCPDs were stable and easily prepared, and the PL emissions could be controlled under higher temperature conditions (100–140 ℃), which might be applied in a high temperature sensing field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The composition and the possible fluorescence mechanism of NCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the composition of NCPDs, FT-IR spectra were employed to analyze the chemical structure changes. As shown in Fig. S5, obvious changes could be observed between blank control groups and NCPDs prepared under different conditions. The obvious peak around 3,441 cm−1 was attributed to the N-H stretching vibration in the amide and the antisymmetric stretching vibration of the primary amine. The peaks at 3,270 and 1,270 cm−1 could be assigned to O-H stretching vibration, and C-O stretching vibration in the carboxyl group, respectively. The absorption at 1,663, 1,636, 1,404, and 620 cm−1 was due to CO stretching vibration, N-H in-plane bending, C-N stretching vibration, and N-H out-plane bending, respectively. It can be seen from the results that the absorption peaks of primary amine decrease gradually, while those of amides and aromatic heterocycle increase gradually.

In order to verify the chemical composition of NCPDs, 1H NMR of the product was tested for further demonstration ( Fig. 4a). There were unreacted carboxyl groups in the products since the chemical shift at 2.6 ppm, which belonged to the methylene group connected with the carboxyl group H, and the basic carbon skeleton of PAMAM was retained. The chemical shift at 5.7 ppm indicated the presence of amide in NCPDs, and the chemical shift at 6.1 ppm belonged to the aromatic heterocycle structure [29].

Then X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was employed to analyze the valence state of element in the NCPDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra showed three peaks at around 284.70, 286.29, 288.56 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, C-N species, respectively. The fitted N 1 s spectrum exhibited three typical components, including aromatic pyrrolic N (400.25 eV), graphitic N (401.39 eV), and oxidized pyrrolic N (402.04 eV) [26], as shown in Fig. 4c. These results further confirmed that the carbon core was mainly composed of the N-doped carbon structure of graphene-like materials. Furthermore, a strong absorption peak at 236 nm belonged to the π→π * transition of sp2-hybrid carbon, and a peak at around 350 nm was attributed to the n→π * transition in the heteroatom (N or O) of amide or carboxylic acid (Fig. S4).

There were abundant small p-π conjugated structures in the surface groups of NCPDs, which contained more active delocalized electrons and requires lower energy for electron transition. While the inside carbon core contained a lot of conjugated π bonds, and the electronic structure was relatively stable, so the energy required for transition was higher. When the sub-fluorescent groups were uniformly and stably distributed on the surface of the carbon cores, the carbon cores limited the vibration and rotation of these groups, enhancing the fluorescence radiation. The chemical structures in the carbon core could interact with surface groups to produce electron-hole composite radiation [30,31]. Therefore, the surface states and the carbon core states co-dominate the fluorescence emission of the NCPDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influencing factors on the fluorescence properties of NCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: It’s important that NCPDs can adapt to different chemical environments. The fluorescence stability and photo bleaching were then studied by monitoring fluorescence spectra. The NCPDs was measured by diluting in buffer solution with a pH of 2–12. Then these diluted solutions were kept at 35 ℃ for a 20-days fluorescence stability test (Fig. S6a). The fluorescence emissions of NCPDs were strongly affected by the pH. It was favorable for the fluorescence emission of NCPDs in a weak alkaline environment, and the maximum value appears at pH = 8 (Fig. S6b). The pH-dependent behavior is the result of the changes in the surface state induced by protonation-deprotonation. The fluorescence intensities under different pH conditions were reduced by 9–48 % after 20 days at 35 ℃. The fluorescence intensity reduction rate was in the range of 9–23 % under mild conditions. Then the photo bleaching test of NCPDs is conducted to examine its practical application potential. Three parts of NCPDs were diluted with pH= 8 buffer solution to the same concentration and placed in the environment of tinfoil shading, natural light irradiation and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation, respectively. The photo bleaching test was conducted at room temperature for 6 h (Fig. S6c). The fluorescence intensity of NCPDs solution treated with tinfoil shading was almost unchanged. The fluorescence intensity of NCPDs solution irradiated by natural light was only reduced by about 2 %, while that irradiated by 365 nm was almost completely quenched (reduced by 89 %). Also, the Zeta potential of NCPDs was 5.87 mV at pH 5.01, but reduced to 2.42 mV at pH 7.22, which was caused by the protonation of surface amino groups and the acidification of sodium citrate. The pH-dependent stability estimation of NCPDs was consistent with the fluorescence properties (Fig. S7).

Due to the large number of polar functional groups (carboxyl, amine, and amide) that distributed on the surface, the solubility of NCPDs in polar solvent was excellent, and NCPDs could be extracted from the aqueous phase even with weakly polar acetone. After the solids were removed by centrifugation, weak fluorescence could be observed from the remaining mixture (Fig. S8). During the experiments, we found that the polarity and type of the solvent can affect the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs. We studied the effect of isopropanol, acetone, methanol, and ethylene glycol on the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent systems (Fig. S9). In the mixed solvent systems of isopropanol-water, acetone-water, methanol-water, and glycol-water, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs showed a trend of increasing firstly and then decreasing, while in the highly polar glycol-water system, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs always showed an increasing trend. It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity showed a precipice decline at a certain proportion of mixed solvents in the isopropanol-water and acetone-water systems. At the same time, the experimental results also showed that a part of NCPDs solids would precipitate. The fluorescence emission of NCPDs was affected by the protonation degree of the surface group and the polarity of the solvent. These two effects were opposite and in a competitive relationship. Low degree of protonation and high polarity were both favorable to NCPDs fluorescence radiation.

Many studies have shown that the aggregation-caused quenching exists widely in fluorescent materials including CDs [32,33]. In this work, the NCPDs is stable, the linear concentration range of NCPDs should be identified in practical applications. At the same time, the quenching effect of self-absorption quenching caused by the partial overlap of the emission spectrum and the absorption spectrum of NCPDs was observed, which is resemble be similar to the researches [34,35]. From the concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra, the fluorescence intensity showed a strict colligative relationship with the concentration of NCPDs, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9995 when the concentration of NCPDs in the aqueous phase was from 0.125 to 3 mM (calculated by the concentration of PAMAM in the solution) ( Fig. 5a). As the concentration increased continually, the increase in fluorescence intensity tended to be slower. Thus, the NCPDs concentration of 3 mM is important and suitable in practical applications.

Owing to a good water solubility and stable fluorescent property of NCPDs, the metal ions detection of the NCPDs was performed in water with different metal cations, including alkali metal ions (Na+, K+ and Ag+), alkaline earth metal ions (Ca2+), and heavy metal ions (Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+). Figure Fig. 5b showed that the fluorescence intensities of Hg2+, Ni2+and Ag+ slightly decreased, and Cu2+ greatly quenched the fluorescence, suggesting that Cu2+ ions have a strong quenching effect on NCPDs. The characterization results showed that NCPDs contained a large number of functional groups with lone electrons, which are prone to complexation reactions. We believed that the reason for Cu2+-induced quenching was the special coordination interaction between Cu2+ ions and functional groups in NCPDs, in other words electron transfer [36]. We further explored the practical significance of NCPDs for Cu2+ detection. Fig. 5c showed the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs and concentrations of Cu2+. Here the Cu2+-induced quenching was analyzed by using the equation: F0-F=KC. Where F0 and F are the intensities of the NCPDs before and after the adding of quencher (Cu2+), respectively, and C is the concentration of the quencher (Cu2+), K is the fluorescence quenching constant. After linear fitting, it is found that F0-F showed a good linear correlation versus the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 80 μM. The regression equation was F0-F= 4.311 C+ 12.33 with the correlation coefficient of R2 2 = 0.9971.

And the detection limit (LOD) was 9 nM (S/N = 3), which is much lower than the safety limits (20 μM) in drinking water and the previous reports [18–23,37]. Compared with the results of previous studies, it also has obvious advantages in the LOD, as shown in Table S2. No quenched fluorescence of NCPDs could be observed with common alkali metals and alkaline earth metals, and partial transition metals. It is worth noting that the fluorescence quenching could be also observed by adding Hg2+ with a tiny change of the fluorescence position (red-shifted about 3 nm). Therefore, copper ion can be clearly distinguished in detection. In the detection of simulated water samples with different ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+), the recovery of Cu2+ was 102.3–106.5 % (Table S3), which showed strong anti-interference ability and potential application.

The zeta potential of NCPDs was also measured after the detection of Cu2+ at pH 7.22, and the value increased to a higher positive value (3.28 mV, 4.06 mV with 10 μM, 80 μM Cu2+, respectively). At the same time, the particle size of the NCPDs was slightly larger after adding 80 μM Cu2+. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs was 7.70 ns, while the Cu2+ quenching did not reduce the fluorescence lifetime (7.57 ns with 10 μM Cu2+, 7.08 ns with 80 μM Cu2+), indicating that the carbon core states with higher excitation energy and the n-π conjugation of PAMAM polymer surface states with lower energy are stable, Cu2+ could not change the chemical structure of NCPDs ( Fig. 6). The carbonyl group and the NH2 groups presented at the surface of NCPDs, which enhanced the complexing ability of the smaller radius Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+ quenching occurs on the surface, and complexation occurs between the vacant orbital and Cu2+ ions [38]. Thus, the electronic transition was forbidden from the orbital to the HOMO level, resulting in significant static fluorescence quenching.

In summary, bright blue fluorescent NCPDs with excitation-independent emission were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method based on the PAMAM and Na3Cit. An in-depth study shows that the surface states and carbon core states produce fluorescence emission together. The fluorescence quantum yield of NCPDs is up to 57 %, and their blue fluorescence can be observed in sunlight. These NCPDs have great chemical stability and photo stability, while they are sensitive to the pH value of the environment, which could provide more applications. Furthermore, NCPDs show remarkable performance in detecting Cu2+ with detection limit of 9 nM, which is much lower than the international standard (μM level). Therefore, the present work provides a new type of NCPDs with surface and carbon core states, shows great potential in Cu2+ detection. Moreover, two fluorescence excitation sites NCPDs are found to control the PL emissions properies from 100 to 140 ℃, which is also a remarkable candidate of high temperature sensing material.

Xiaoyi Lu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shangshang Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Weiguang Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Dan Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hong-Wei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shihan Xu: Writing – review & editing. Biao Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82073475), Major Basic Research Projects of Shandong Natural Science Foundation (ZR2020ZD36) and the Graduate Student Innovation Research Project of Northeast Petroleum University (JYCX_CX03_2018). We are grateful to the research group of Xiaoyang Liu (Jilin University), microscopic test center of the Northeast Petroleum University for characterization, and the Daqing Ecological Environment Monitoring Center for environmental assessment.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129441.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
